mappings.rationaleAI: Directly addresses museums and galleries (A.1), including the management and maintenance of institutional collections (A.1.a) and types of museum categorized by subject area including art, natural history, science, and history (A.1.b).

Directly addresses libraries (A.2, B.4), including types of libraries — public, academic, national, research, special, digital (B.4.a) — and the science of library systems including acquisition, organization, and circulation services (B.4.b).
News dissemination through print, broadcasting, and digital media connects to newspapers (C.3.a.ii) and broadcasting through radio and television (C.4).
AI systems performing search, recommendation, and natural language processing connect to bibliographic and numeric databases (B.6) as computational tools for storage and retrieval of information.

Gutenberg's invention of the European movable-type printing press and the Gutenberg Bible as the first mechanically printed book directly connect to print publishing of books (C.3.a.i), catalyzing mass-production of texts.

Broadcasting as one-to-many distribution of audio or audiovisual content directly maps to broadcasting (C.4), including radio (C.4.a) and television (C.4.b), with coverage of commercial, public, and community models.

Publishing as the process of making content publicly available directly maps to selection, preparation, and marketing of printed material (C.3), including print publishing of books, newspapers, and periodicals (C.3.a) and electronic publishing (C.3.b).

Books as written works distributed in printed, audio, or electronic form, with their evolution from clay tablets to codex manuscripts and machine-printed volumes, directly connect to print publishing of books (C.3.a.i).
Paper as the medium for printed material, invented in Han dynasty China and refined through chemical and mechanical pulping, connects to print publishing (C.3.a) as the foundational substrate.

Education as the transmission of knowledge through formal and informal institutions directly connects to educational institutions (C.1) as institutions for the advancement and dissemination of knowledge.
The history of science encompassing institutional history (universities, journals, societies) connects to academies of learning or societies established for the advancement of knowledge (C.2).

Mass media comprising broadcast, print, digital, and streaming channels connects to publishing (C.3) and broadcasting (C.4) as institutions for disseminating knowledge and information to large audiences.
Science as systematic knowledge-building through institutional collaborative research connects to academies of learning and societies established for the advancement of knowledge (C.2).
Literature as written and oral works distributed through printing and digital publication connects to print publishing of books (C.3.a.i).

Research as the systematic process of increasing knowledge through evidence collection, peer review, and publication connects to academies of learning and societies for the advancement of knowledge (C.2).

The Islamic Golden Age's intellectual production through Abbasid caliphate patronage and urban centers of learning (Baghdad, Cordoba, Cairo) represents historical academies and societies for the advancement of knowledge (C.2).

The Enlightenment's dissemination through encyclopedias (B.2) and the print trade (C.3.a), and its institutional basis in salons, coffeehouses, and learned societies (C.2), directly connect to knowledge preservation and dissemination.

Oral tradition as the transmission of knowledge through speech across generations represents a foundational mode of storage and retrieval of information (B), predating written records and continuing alongside them.

Shen Kuo's Dream Pool Essays recording scientific observations across mathematics, optics, and cartography exemplifies historical knowledge preservation through learned societies and imperial patronage (C.2).

The history of technology tracing computing and communication technologies connects to museums of science and technology (A.1.b.iii), and the history of printing technology connects to print publishing (C.3.a).

Computers as programmable machines foundational to the Digital Revolution connect to bibliographic and numeric databases (B.6) and electronic publishing (C.3.b) as tools for information storage, retrieval, and dissemination.
